mnemonics.title: SMILE for String Methods
mnemonics.content: S-Substring, M-Modify via new object, I-Immutable nature, L-Length preserved, E-Existing strings unchanged. Remembers key string modification concepts

mnemonics.title: CRS Alert
mnemonics.content: Concat -> Replace -> Substring: Three amigos that always create NEW strings. Visualize three mariachi musicians creating new songs

mnemonics.title: Builder's Triangle
mnemonics.content: Three sides of efficient modification: 1) Use StringBuilder for single-thread 2) StringBuffer for threads 3) Never use + in loops

mnemonics.title: STRING Steps
mnemonics.content: S: Select method | T: Transform to char array if needed | R: Return new object | I: Inspect original remains | N: Never modify original | G: Generate result

acronyms.usage: Reminds to use StringBuilder instead of + operator in loops
Methods that force new string creation: Concat, Replace, Substring
Remember Strings are immutable; use Builder/Buffer for changes

visualTips.visualization: Imagine strings as granite tombstones - you can make rubbings (new copies) with added markings, but original remains unchanged
Picture a whiteboard - you can erase/modify content freely without creating new boards each time
Visualize a library where popular books (strings) are shared. Modifying = writing in a book? No! You get a new copy instead
Imagine building a brick wall: Using + operator = dismantling and rebuilding entire wall each time. StringBuilder = adding bricks directly

analogies.analogy: Like family heirloom jewelry - you can make copies with alterations, but the original pieces remain forever unchanged
Office whiteboard (StringBuilder) vs Bank vault whiteboard (StringBuffer). One needs no security, the other requires thread-safe locks
Like a restaurant menu - common dishes (strings) are prepared once and served to multiple customers (references) efficiently
